cat(paste0(length(unique(fit$data$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, fig.cap = "Summary of fit indicators. R-hat should be smaller than 1.01 and the effect sample size (ess) should be larger than 500.", fig.height=3}
sample_diags = fit %>% model_summary()
sample_diags$plot
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("<details> \n <summary>Click to show a table with the complete sample diagnostics.</summary>")
  cat(sample_diags$table)
  cat("</details> \n <br>")
}
```

We calculate the contrasts manually from posterior predictions next:

```{r fig.height=2.5, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of estimated first stress reinstatement effects on self-reported self-consciousness on a 0-100 VAS within and across men and women, as well as the difference between sexes (women-men). Density plots show posterior distributions. Dots and lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
tmp = plot_prepost_contrast(fit, the_data,"self-conscious (1st reinstatement)", my_contrast = "stress")
tmp[[1]]+ggtitle("1st stress reminder effect on self-consciousness")+xlab("Stress effect: stress (post - pre) - control (post - pre)")
```

```{r }
tmp$table
post = tmp[[2]]
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
write_stress_effect(post,"*the first state reinstatement* on change in self-consciousness ratings (post-pre)")
```

### Stressed

We also repeat the same analysis for stress ratings.

```{r StateReinstStresses, fig.cap= "Embarrassment ratings over time. Lines are mean responses. Ribbons are 95% within-subjects confidence intervals. Alarms and syringes indicate state inductions/reinstatements and drug administrations, repsectively.", results='hide',fig.keep='all'}
my_data.Q.stre = select_data(c("stressed"))
my_stages = 5:6
my_data.Q.stre %>% plot_stages(my_stages=my_stages) + scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0,0,0,1.75))
```

```{r, fig.height=3, fig.cap="Change in stress ratings from pre to post 1st state reinstatement. Each line represents an individual. The state induction effect is reflected in the slope of the lines."}
the_data = make_the_data(my_data.Q.stre, phase = "reminder1")[, outcome := "selfc"]

the_data %>% plot_pre_post("reminder") + scale_col_stress
```

```{r, fig.height=3, fig.cap="Distribution of differences in the change in stress ratings from pre to post 1st state reinstatement, stratified by drug condition (panels) and sex (colors).", results='hide',fig.keep='all'}
plot_stresseff_raw(the_data)+ggtitle("1st stress reminder effect on stress rating")+xlab("Stress effect: stress (post - pre) - control (post - pre)")
```

```{r, fig.height=3, fig.cap="Mean and 95% confidence intervals of differences in the change in stress ratings from pre to post 1st state reinstatement between the stressful and the control reinstatement, stratified by drug condition (colors) and sex (panels). Note that these are calculated from the raw data."}
plot_stresseff_raw2(the_data)+ylab("Effect of 1st stress reminder")
```

Here we run the Bayesian ordinal logistic regression:

```{r fig.cap="Posterior predictive check"}
fn = "brmsP1/Reminder/stressed1_acat.Rdata"
the_data = the_data %>% 
  coarsen(var = "response", bins = 20, range = c(0,100))
if (file.exists(fn)) {
  load(fn)
} else {
  fit = fit_ordinal_model(the_data, stress_formula, fn, family = acat())
}
#pp_check(fit, ndraws = 2000, type = "bars")
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(fit$data$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, fig.cap = "Summary of fit indicators. R-hat should be smaller than 1.01 and the effect sample size (ess) should be larger than 500.", fig.height=3}
sample_diags = fit %>% model_summary()
sample_diags$plot
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("<details> \n <summary>Click to show a table with the complete sample diagnostics.</summary>")
  cat(sample_diags$table)
  cat("</details> \n <br>")
}
```

We calculate the contrasts manually from posterior predictions:

```{r fig.height=2.5, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of estimated first stress reinstatement effects on self-reported stress on a 0-100 VAS within and across men and women, as well as the difference between sexes (women-men). Density plots show posterior distributions. Dots and lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
tmp = plot_prepost_contrast(fit, the_data,"stressed (1st reinstatement)", my_contrast = "stress")
tmp[[1]]+ggtitle("1st stress reminder effect on stress rating")+xlab("Stress effect: stress (post - pre) - control (post - pre)")
```

```{r }
tmp$table
post = tmp[[2]]
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
write_stress_effect(post,"*the first state reinstatement* on change in stress ratings (post-pre)")
```

## Both state reinstatement videos

The following analyses show state reinstatement effects across both reinstatement (or *reminder*) videos.

<!-- # {.tabset} -->

### Embarrassed

```{r StateReinstEmb2Vids, fig.height=7, fig.cap="Change in embarrassment ratings from pre to post state reinstatement. A) Embarrassment ratings over time. Lines are mean responses. B) Each line represents an individual. The state reinstatement effect is reflected in the slope of the lines. C) Mean and 95% confidence intervals of differences in the change in embarrassment ratings from pre to post 1st state reinstatement between the stressful and the control reinstatement, stratified by drug condition (colors) and sex (panels). Note that these are calculated from the raw data.", results='hide',fig.keep='all'}
my_data.Q.emb = select_data(c("embarassed"))
my_stages = 5:8
p1 = my_data.Q.emb %>% plot_stages(my_stages = my_stages) + xlim(15,45)
the_data = make_the_data(my_data.Q.emb, phase = "reminder")[, outcome := "emb"]

p2 = the_data %>% plot_pre_post("reminder") + scale_col_stress
p3 = plot_stresseff_raw2(the_data) + theme(legend.position = "right")+ylab("Effect of stress reminders")
(p1 | p2) / p3 + plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")
```

Here we add a distinction between second and first state reinstatement to the model:

```{r class.source = 'fold-show'}
stress_formula = 
  response.c ~ induced.state:stage:Sex:reminder.order + 
               session +
               (1+induced.state + stage + induced.state + stage|participant)
```

```{r fig.cap="Posterior predictive check"}
fn = "brmsP1/Reminder/emb2_acat.Rdata"
the_data = the_data %>% 
  coarsen(var = "response", bins = 20, range = c(0,100)) %>%
  .[, reminder.order := factor(ifelse(orig.stage<7,1,2))]
if (file.exists(fn)) {
  load(fn)
} else {
  fit = fit_ordinal_model(the_data, stress_formula, fn, family = acat(),
                          warmup = 1000, iter = 3000, sd.prior = prior(gamma(1.1,.1), class = "sd"))
}
#pp_check(fit, ndraws = 2000, type = "bars")
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(fit$data$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, fig.cap = "Summary of fit indicators. R-hat should be smaller than 1.01 and the effect sample size (ess) should be larger than 500.", fig.height=3}
sample_diags = fit %>% model_summary()
sample_diags$plot
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("<details> \n <summary>Click to show a table with the complete sample diagnostics.</summary>")
  cat(sample_diags$table)
  cat("</details> \n <br>")
}
```

We calculate the contrasts manually from posterior predictions as before:

```{r fig.height=2.5, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of estimated stress reinstatement effects on self-reported embarrassment on a 0-100 VAS within and across men and women, as well as the difference between sexes (women-men). Density plots show posterior distributions. Dots and lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
tmp = plot_prepost_contrast(fit, the_data,"embarrassed (2 reminders)", my_contrast = "stress")
tmp[[1]]+ggtitle("Stress reminder effect on embarrassment")+xlab("Stress effect: stress (post - pre) - control (post - pre)")
```

```{r }
tmp$table
post = tmp[[2]]
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
write_stress_effect(post,"*both state reinstatements* on change in embarrassment ratings (post-pre)")
```

### Self-concious

We repeat the analysis of self-conscious ratings, now taking into account that there are two state reinstatements.

```{r StateReinstSelfConf2Vids, fig.height=7, fig.cap="Change in self-consciousness ratings from pre to post state reinstatement. A) Self-consciousness ratings over time. Lines are mean responses. B) Each line represents an individual. The state reinstatement effect is reflected in the slope of the lines. C) Mean and 95% confidence intervals of differences in the change in self-consciousness ratings from pre to post 1st state reinstatement between the stressful and the control reinstatement, stratified by drug condition (colors) and sex (panels). Note that these are calculated from the raw data.", results='hide',fig.keep='all'}
my_data.Q.emb = select_data(c("selfconscious"))
my_stages = 5:8
p1 = my_data.Q.emb %>% plot_stages(my_stages = my_stages) + xlim(15,45)
the_data = make_the_data(my_data.Q.emb, phase = "reminder")[, outcome := "emb"]

p2 = the_data %>% plot_pre_post("reminder") + scale_col_stress
p3 = plot_stresseff_raw2(the_data) + theme(legend.position = "right")+ylab("Effect of stress reminders")
(p1 | p2) / p3 + plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")
```

Here we run the Bayesian ordinal logistic regression:

```{r fig.cap="Posterior predictive check"}
fn = "brmsP1/Reminder/selfc2_acat.Rdata"
the_data = the_data %>% 
  coarsen(var = "response", bins = 20, range = c(0,100)) %>%
  .[, reminder.order := factor(ifelse(orig.stage<7,1,2))]
if (file.exists(fn)) {
  load(fn)
} else {
  fit = fit_ordinal_model(the_data, stress_formula, fn,family = acat(),
                          warmup = 1000, iter = 3000)
}
#pp_check(fit, ndraws = 2000, type = "bars")
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(fit$data$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, fig.cap = "Summary of fit indicators. R-hat should be smaller than 1.01 and the effect sample size (ess) should be larger than 500.", fig.height=3}
sample_diags = fit %>% model_summary()
sample_diags$plot
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("<details> \n <summary>Click to show a table with the complete sample diagnostics.</summary>")
  cat(sample_diags$table)
  cat("</details> \n <br>")
}
```

We calculate the contrasts manually from posterior predictions:

```{r fig.height=2.5, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of estimated stress reinstatement effects on self-reported self-consciousness on a 0-100 VAS within and across men and women, as well as the difference between sexes (women-men). Density plots show posterior distributions. Dots and lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
tmp = plot_prepost_contrast(fit, the_data,"self-conscious (2 reminders)", my_contrast = "stress")
tmp[[1]]+ggtitle("Stress reminder effect on self-consciousness")+xlab("Stress effect: stress (post - pre) - control (post - pre)")
```

```{r }
tmp$table
post = tmp[[2]]
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
write_stress_effect(post,"*both state reinstatements* on change in self-consciousness ratings (post-pre)")
```

### Stressed

We repeat the analysis as for stress while also taking into account that there are two reminders.

```{r StateReinstStresses2Vids, fig.height=7, fig.cap="Change in stress ratings from pre to post state reinstatement. A) Stress ratings over time. Lines are mean responses. B) Each line represents an individual. The state reinstatement effect is reflected in the slope of the lines. C) Mean and 95% confidence intervals of differences in the change in stress ratings from pre to post 1st state reinstatement between the stressful and the control reinstatement, stratified by drug condition (colors) and sex (panels). Note that these are calculated from the raw data.", results='hide',fig.keep='all'}
my_data.Q.emb = select_data(c("stressed"))
my_stages = 5:8
p1 = my_data.Q.emb %>% plot_stages(my_stages = my_stages) + xlim(15,45)
the_data = make_the_data(my_data.Q.emb, phase = "reminder")[, outcome := "emb"]

p2 = the_data %>% plot_pre_post("reminder") + scale_col_stress
p3 = plot_stresseff_raw2(the_data) + theme(legend.position = "right")+ylab("Effect of stress reminders")
(p1 | p2) / p3 + plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")
```

Here we run the Bayesian ordinal logistic regression:

```{r fig.cap="Posterior predictive check"}
fn = "brmsP1/Reminder/stressed2_acat.Rdata"
the_data = the_data %>% 
  coarsen(var = "response", bins = 20, range = c(0,100)) %>%
  .[, reminder.order := factor(ifelse(orig.stage<7,1,2))]
if (file.exists(fn)) {
  load(fn)
} else {
  fit = fit_ordinal_model(the_data, stress_formula, fn, family = acat(),
                          warmup = 1000, iter = 3000)
}
#pp_check(fit, ndraws = 2000, type = "bars")
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(fit$data$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, fig.cap = "Summary of fit indicators. R-hat should be smaller than 1.01 and the effect sample size (ess) should be larger than 500.", fig.height=3}
sample_diags = fit %>% model_summary()
sample_diags$plot
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("<details> \n <summary>Click to show a table with the complete sample diagnostics.</summary>")
  cat(sample_diags$table)
  cat("</details> \n <br>")
}
```

We calculate the contrasts manually from posterior predictions:

```{r fig.height=2.5, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of estimated stress reinstatement effects on self-reported stress on a 0-100 VAS within and across men and women, as well as the difference between sexes (women-men). Density plots show posterior distributions. Dots and lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
tmp = plot_prepost_contrast(fit, the_data,"stressed (2 reminders)", my_contrast = "stress")
tmp[[1]]+ggtitle("Stress reminder effect on stress rating")+xlab("Stress effect: stress (post - pre) - control (post - pre)")
```

```{r }
tmp$table
post = tmp[[2]]
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
write_stress_effect(post,"*both state reinstatements* on change in stress ratings (post-pre)")
```

# Drug manipulation check

## High

```{r DrugManCheckHigh, fig.cap= "Feeling high over time. Lines are mean responses. Ribbons are 95% within-subjects confidence intervals."}
my_data.Q.high = select_data(c("high"))
my_stages = 1:11
my_data.Q.high %>% 
  .[stage %in% my_stages] %>% 
    .[,.(m = mean(response), se = sd(response)/sqrt(length(unique(participant)))), 
      by = .(stage,Sex,Drug)] %>% 
    .[, `:=`(lower = m-2*se, upper = m+2*se)] %>% 
    ggplot(aes(x = stage, y = m, color = Drug, lty = Sex, fill = Drug)) + 
    geom_line() + 
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = .2, color = NA) + 
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = my_stages, labels = stage.names[my_stages]) +
  scale_col_drug
```

```{r, fig.height=3, fig.cap="Change in high ratings from pre to post first drug administration. Each line represents an individual. The drug effect is reflected in the slope of the lines."}
the_data = make_the_data(my_data.Q.high[stage %in% 4:5],phase = "drug")[, outcome := "high"]
the_data %>% plot_pre_post("drug")
```

We can further summarize this information by showing the distribution of the slopes in the previous figure:

```{r, fig.height=3, fig.cap="Distribution of differences in the change in high ratings from pre to post first drug administration between the oxycodone and placebo conditions, stratified by state induction condition (panels) and sex (colors)."}
plot_drug_effect_raw(the_data)+ggtitle("Oxycodone effect on 'high' rating")+xlab("Oxycodone effect: oxycodone (post - pre) - placebo (post - pre)")
```

Here we run the Bayesian ordinal logistic regression:

```{r fig.cap="Posterior predictive check"}
fn = "brmsP1/high_acat.Rdata"
the_data = the_data %>%
  .[, Drug := factor(Drug, levels = c("placebo","oxycodone"))] %>% 
  coarsen(var = "response", bins = 20, range = c(0,100))
drug_formula = response.c ~ Drug:stage:Sex + (1 | session) + (1 | participant) 
#drug_formula = response.c ~ Drug:stage:Sex + (1 | session) + (1 + Drug:stage | participant)
if (file.exists(fn)) {
  load(fn)
} else {
  fit = fit_ordinal_model(the_data, drug_formula, fn, family = acat())
}
#pp_check(fit, ndraws = 2000, type = "bars")
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(fit$data$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, fig.cap = "Summary of fit indicators. R-hat should be smaller than 1.01 and the effect sample size (ess) should be larger than 500.", fig.height=3}
sample_diags = fit %>% model_summary()
sample_diags$plot
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("<details> \n <summary>Click to show a table with the complete sample diagnostics.</summary>")
  cat(sample_diags$table)
  cat("</details> \n <br>")
}
```

We calculate the contrasts manually from posterior predictions:

```{r fig.height=2.5, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of estimated drug effects on self-reported high on a 0-100 VAS within and across men and women, as well as the difference between sexes (women-men). Density plots show posterior distributions. Dots and lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
tmp = plot_prepost_contrast(fit, the_data, "high", my_contrast = "drug")
tmp[[1]]+ggtitle("Oxycodone effect on 'high' rating")+xlab("Oxycodone effect: oxycodone (post - pre) - placebo (post - pre)")
```

```{r }
tmp$table
post = tmp[[2]]
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
write_drug_effect = function(post, outcome = "being high") {
  cat(paste0(
  "The estimated effect of drug on change in ratings of ",outcome," from pre to post first drug administration is ",
  print_effect(post[Sex == "total", value]),
  ". The effect is ",
  print_effect(post[Sex == "women", value]),
  " in women and ",
  print_effect(post[Sex == "men", value]),
  " in men, and the sex difference is ",
  print_effect(post[Sex == "Sex difference", value]),"."
  ))
  }
write_drug_effect(post, outcome="feeling high")
```


```{r, echo = FALSE, results='asis'}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("## Drug side effects  {.tabset}")
} else {
  #cat("## Drug side effects")
}
```

```{r echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, results = "asis"}
for (var in sideffect_items){
  show.details = ifelse(var == sideffect_items[1],TRUE,FALSE)
  out <- knit_child("drug_side.Rmd", quiet = TRUE)
  cat(out)
  cat("\n\n")
}
```

```{r eval = FALSE, side_effects,echo=FALSE,message=FALSE,results="asis", fig.height=2.5}
for (var in sideffect_items) {
  cat(paste("\n\n### Side effect: ",var,"\n"))
  fn = paste0("brmsP1/",var,"_acat.Rdata")
  the_data = make_the_data(select_data(c(var)) %>% .[stage %in% 4:5],phase = "drug")[, outcome := var]
  data_plot = the_data %>% plot_pre_post("drug")
  the_data = 
    the_data %>%
    .[, Drug := factor(Drug, levels = c("placebo","oxycodone"))] %>% 
    coarsen(var = "response", bins = 20, range = c(0,100))
  if (file.exists(fn)) {
    load(fn)
  } else {
    fit = fit_ordinal_model(the_data, drug_formula, fn, family = acat())
  }
  tmp = plot_prepost_contrast(fit, the_data, var, my_contrast = "drug")
  cat(paste0("\n\nRaw data for '",var,"'\n\n"))
  print(data_plot)
  cat(paste0("\n\nEstimated effects of Drug for '",var,"'\n\n"))
  print(tmp$plot)
  print(tmp$table)
  cat("  \n")#
  write_drug_effect(tmp[[2]],var)
  cat("  \n")#
}
```

# Drug effects in Q5 and Q6

## Overview

We now take a look at the drug effect data that may be of particular relevance to the amount of drug self-administered -- i.e., the measures collected after the first drug administration ("sampling dose") and before the self-administration taks (i.e., main outcome task).

```{r DrugEffectsQ5Q6, fig.height=8}
my_data.Q.drug56 =
  my_data.Q[,.(participant, session, stage, response, state.condition, Sex, Drug, item_name)] %>% 
  .[, session := factor(session)]

stage.names.b = gsub("\\\n"," ", stage.names)
pdata = 
  my_data.Q.drug56 %>% 
  .[item_name %in% c("drug_like","drug_again","drug_dislike","high")] %>% 
  .[,.(m = mean(response), se = sd(response)/sqrt(length(unique(participant)))), 
    by = .(stage,Sex,state.condition, Drug, item_name)] %>% 
  .[, `:=`(lower = m-2*se, upper = m+2*se)] %>% 
  .[lower < 0, lower := 0]

pdata %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = stage, y = m, color = Drug, lty = Sex, fill = Drug)) + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(4.9,6.1), col = "red") + 
  geom_line() + 
  facet_grid(item_name~state.condition, scale = "free") + 
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = .2, color = NA) + 
  scale_col_drug + 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:11, labels = stage.names.b) + 
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 1, hjust=1))

saveRDS(my_data.Q.drug56,file = "tmp.RDS")

```

```{r, echo = FALSE, results='asis'}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("Click the tabs below to display all ratings pertaining to drug effects averaged across the two timepoints illustrated above.\n")
  cat("\n\n## Drug effects by outcome {.tabset}")
} else {
  #cat("## Drug effects by outcome")
}
```

```{r, echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, results = "asis"}

vars = c("drug_like","drug_dislike", "drug_again","high",sideffect_items,"pos_affect","neg_affect")
var.names = vars %>% gsub("_"," ",.) %>% gsub("like","liking",.) %>% gsub("neg","negative",.) %>% gsub("pos","positive",.)
names(var.names) = vars

drug_effects.stats = c()
drug_effects.stats.b = c()
raw_contrasts.drug = c()

for (var in vars){
  cat(var,file = "current_variable.txt")
  var.name = var.names[var]
  show.details = ifelse(vars == vars[1],TRUE,FALSE)
  out <- knit_child("drug56.Rmd", quiet = TRUE)
  cat(out)
  cat("\n\n")
}
```

```{r eval = FALSE, DrugEffectsQ56, results='asis', echo = FALSE, fig.cap=c("Raw data for the effect of drug.","Posterior distributions for effect of drug.")}

make_tbl(post.contrasts, 
         caption = "Main and interaction effects of Drug on 'drug liking'.") %>%  
  add_footer_lines(c("Single words identify main effects in different conditions or sexes. '-' identifies contasts. 'in' signifies that an effect is reported for a subgroub or condition.","Numbers show drug effects."))

base_caption = "The figure shows Left: MYVAR by sex. Top right: MYVAR by stress condition. Note that MYVAR is the difference in MYVAR between the ocycodone and placebo conditions. Bottom right, clockwise from top left: Sex differences in MYVAR, stress effect on MYVAR, sex difference in MYVAR in the stress condition, sex difference in MYVAR in the control condition (for stress). Bottom right: Difference between the sex differences in MYVAR between the control and stress condition.\n\n"

vars = c("drug_dislike", "drug_again","high",sideffect_items,"pos_affect","neg_affect")
for (var in vars){
  fn = paste0("brmsP1/drug/",gsub("drug_","",var),".Rdata")
  my_caption = gsub("MYVAR",gsub("_"," ",var),base_caption)
  
  drug_formula = response.c ~ state.condition:Sex:Drug + (1|session) + (1|participant/stage) #+ (0+state.condition:Drug|participant)
  the_data = my_data.Q.drug56[item_name == var]
  if (var == "pos_affect") the_data = my_data.Q.drug56[item_name %in% posaffect_items]
  if (var == "neg_affect") the_data = my_data.Q.drug56[item_name %in% negaffect_items]
  if (var %in% c("pos_affect","neg_affect"))
    drug_formula = response.c ~ state.condition:Sex:Drug + (1|session) + (1|participant/stage) + (1|item_name) 
  the_data_orig = copy(the_data)
  if (!(var %in% c("pos_affect","neg_affect"))) {
    raw_contrasts.drug = rbind(
      raw_contrasts.drug,
      plot_drug(the_data)$data[, .(participant, Sex, drug_effect, state.condition)][, Outcome := var]
    )
  }
  if (var == "drug_again") {
    cat("DEQ item: Take drug again ratings")
  } else if (var == "high") {
     cat("Feeling high ratings")
  } else {
    cat(paste("##",stringr::str_to_title(gsub("_"," ",var)),"ratings\n\n"))
  }
  print(the_data %>% plot_drug_lines() + ylab(var))

  the_data = the_data %>% coarsen(var = "response", bins = 20, range = c(0,100))
  the_data[, response.c := ordered(response.c, levels = names(which(table(the_data[, response.c])>0)))]
  if (file.exists(fn)) {
    load(fn)
  } else {
    fit = fit_ordinal_model(the_data, drug_formula, family = acat(), fn)
  }
  post.contrasts = get_drug_contrasts(fit, the_data)
  
  drug_effects.stats = rbind(
    drug_effects.stats,
    get_drug_effects.stats(post.contrasts, var))
  drug_effects.stats.b = rbind(
    drug_effects.stats.b,
    get_drug_effects.stats.b(post.contrasts, var))
  
  attr(post.contrasts,"cell_stats") %>%
    my_flextable(caption = paste("Estimated responses for", var ,"by sex, state condition, drug")) %>%
    flextable_to_rmd()
  
  print(drug_plotter(post.contrasts, ox=gsub("_"," ",var)))
  cat(my_caption)
  
  make_tbl(post.contrasts,
           caption = paste0("Main and interaction effects of drug on '", var,"'.")) %>%
    flextable_to_rmd()
}
```

## Overview of Drug effects

```{r DrugEffectsOverview}
drug_effects.stats %>% 
  my_flextable(caption = paste("Summary of drug effects"))
```


## Correlations between stress effect, self-administration and liking

```{r, fig.cap="Correlations between drug liking and being stressed in stage 5, and the effect obtained in the self-administration task. The main diagnoal shows the histogram for each variable. The upper triangle shows correlation coefficients (Kendall's Tau) while the lower trianle shows the corresponding scatter plots."}
cdata = merge(
my_data.Q[stage == "5" & item_name %in% c("drug_like","stressed") & Drug == "oxycodone", 
          .(participant, Sex, session, Stress, response, item_name)] %>% 
  dcast(participant + session + Sex + Stress ~ item_name, value.var = "response")
,
my_data %>% copy() %>% 
  .[is.na(satisfact), last.trial := ifelse(time == max(time),TRUE,FALSE), by = .(session, participant)] %>% 
  .[last.trial == TRUE & is.na(satisfact)] %>% 
  .[, stage := 0] %>% 
  .[, N.oxy := sum(Drug == "oxycodone"), by = .(participant)] %>% 
  .[N.oxy > 1] %>% 
  .[, .(participant, session, effect_obtained)],
by = c("participant","session")) %>% 
  .[, Stress := factor(Stress,levels = c("stress","control"))] %>% 
  .[, Sex := factor(Sex, levels = c("women","men"))]


ggpairs(cdata,
        columns = c("drug_like", "stressed", "effect_obtained"),
        mapping =  aes(color = Stress),
        diag = list(continuous = wrap("barDiag", bins = 20)),
        upper = list(continuous = my_cor))
```

```{r, fig.cap="Correlations between drug liking and being stressed in stage 5, and the effect obtained in the self-administration task, in the stress condition. The main diagnoal shows the histogram for each variable. The upper triangle shows correlation coefficients (Kendall's Tau) while the lower trianle shows the corresponding scatter plots."}
ggpairs(cdata[Stress == "stress"],
        columns = c("drug_like", "stressed", "effect_obtained"),
        mapping =  aes(color = Sex),
        diag = list(continuous = wrap("barDiag", bins = 20)),
        upper = list(continuous = my_cor))
```

```{r, fig.cap="Correlations between drug liking and being stressed in stage 5, and the effect obtained in the self-administration task, in the control condition. The main diagnoal shows the histogram for each variable. The upper triangle shows correlation coefficients (Kendall's Tau) while the lower trianle shows the corresponding scatter plots."}
ggpairs(cdata[Stress == "control"],
        columns = c("drug_like", "stressed", "effect_obtained"),
        mapping =  aes(color = Sex),
        diag = list(continuous = wrap("barDiag", bins = 20)),
        upper = list(continuous = my_cor))
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(cdata$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

### Correlations between stress and opioid responses

Here we compute correlations between heart rate, HRV and cortisol stress responses, and select opioid self-administration and subjective opioid effects. This exploratory analysis was restricted to the *stress indiction* \* *oxycodone* condition. 

```{r}
hrhrv_cdata <- read.csv("data/HRHRVdata.csv") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(participant=as.numeric(gsub("E", "", Participant)),
                Drug=tolower(Drug),
                state.condition=tolower(Stress)) %>%
  dplyr::select(participant, Drug, state.condition, Activity, BPM_average, RMSSD) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols=c(BPM_average, RMSSD), names_to="outcome_x", values_to="value_x") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(outcome_x=paste0(outcome_x, " (", Activity, ")")) %>%
  dplyr::select(-Activity)

cortisol_cdata <- my_data.cortisol %>%
  dplyr::mutate(outcome_x=paste0("cortisol_mcgdl (blood_sample_", dplyr::case_match(stage, 2~1, 3~2, 4~3, 6~4, 8~5, 10~6), ")"),
                value_x=concentr) %>%
  dplyr::filter(Drug=="oxycodone" & state.condition=="stress" & outcome_x=="cortisol_mcgdl (blood_sample_3)") %>%
  dplyr::select(participant, Drug, state.condition, outcome_x, value_x)

selfadmin_cdata <- my_data %>% 
  .[is.na(satisfact), last.trial := ifelse(time == max(time),TRUE,FALSE), by = .(session, participant)] %>% 
  .[last.trial == TRUE & is.na(satisfact)] %>% 
  .[, stage := 0] %>% 
  .[, N.oxy := sum(Drug == "oxycodone"), by = .(participant)] %>% 
  .[N.oxy > 1] %>% 
  .[, .(participant, Drug, state.condition, session, effect_obtained)] %>%
  dplyr::mutate(outcome_y="self_admin (effect_obtained)",
                value_y=effect_obtained) %>%
  dplyr::filter(Drug=="oxycodone" & state.condition=="stress") %>%
  dplyr::select(participant, Drug, state.condition, outcome_y, value_y)

quest_cdata <- my_data.Q %>%
  dplyr::filter(Drug=="oxycodone" & state.condition=="stress" & item_name %in% c("high", "drug_like", "drug_dislike") & stage %in% c(5,6)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(outcome_y=item_name) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(participant, Drug, state.condition, outcome_y) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(value_y=mean(response), .groups="drop") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(outcome_y=paste0(outcome_y, " (average_Q5_Q6)"))

summary_cdata <- merge(rbind(hrhrv_cdata, cortisol_cdata),
                       rbind(selfadmin_cdata, quest_cdata),
                       by=c("participant", "Drug", "state.condition"), keep="all") %>%
  dplyr::group_by(Drug, state.condition, outcome_x, outcome_y) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(n=dplyr::n(),
            r=cor.test(value_x, value_y, method="kendall")$estimate,
            p=cor.test(value_x, value_y, method="kendall")$p.value,
            .groups="drop") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Drug=stringr::str_to_sentence(Drug),
    state.condition=stringr::str_to_sentence(state.condition),
    outcome_x=dplyr::case_match(outcome_x,
      "BPM_average (state_mainmath)"~"Heart rate (stress induction)",
      "RMSSD (state_mainmath)"~"Heart rate variability (stress induction)",
      "cortisol_mcgdl (blood_sample_3)"~"Cortisol (post stress induction)"),
    outcome_y=dplyr::case_match(outcome_y,
      "drug_dislike (average_Q5_Q6)"~"Drug disliking (post 1st drug)",
        "drug_like (average_Q5_Q6)"~"Drug liking (post 1st drug)",
      "high (average_Q5_Q6)"~"High (post 1st drug)",
      "self_admin (effect_obtained)"~"Effect obtained (self-admin)")) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(outcome_x=factor(outcome_x, levels=c("Heart rate (stress induction)", "Heart rate variability (stress induction)", "Cortisol (post stress induction)")),
                outcome_y=factor(outcome_y, levels=c("Effect obtained (self-admin)", "High (post 1st drug)", "Drug liking (post 1st drug)", "Drug disliking (post 1st drug)"))) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(outcome_x, outcome_y) %>%
  dplyr::rename(`State condition`=state.condition, `Outcome X`=outcome_x, `Outcome Y`=outcome_y, `Tau`=r)

my_flextable(summary_cdata, caption="Correlations between physiological stress responses and oxycodone effects", digits=3, 
             footnote="Kendall's Tau. Heart rate: Average BPM during state induction. Heart rate variability: RMSSD during state induction. Cortisol: Plasma cortisol (mcg/dl) immediately after state induction. Drug disliking/Drug disliking/High: Average 0-100 VAS rating after 1st drug administration and before self-administration task. Effect obtained: Drug effect obtained in self-administration task.")

```

# Summary of Drug effects

## Time courses of drug effects

The following figure shows means and 95% credible intervals from non-parametric Bayesian models. We label models non-parametric, because each combination of drug condition, sex condition, sex and time point is modeled with its own parameter. The Bayesian estimation also accounts for repeated measurements and session effects.

```{r TimeCourseDrugEffects, fig.height=5, out.width="120%", fig.cap="Time courses for feeling high, drug liking and positive affect spit by drug and state condition. Alarms and syringes indicate state inductions/reinstatements and drug administrations, repsectively."}

p_posaffect = 
  plot_tc_OR_bayes(posaffect_items,"Drug") + 
  ylab("Positive affect (VAS 0-100)") + 
  guides(fill=guide_legend(title="Drug"), shape=guide_legend(title="State induction"))
p_high = plot_tc_OR_bayes("high", "Drug") + 
  ylab("Feeling high (VAS 0-100)") + 
  theme(legend.position = "none") 
p_drug_like = plot_tc_OR_bayes("drug_like", "Drug") + 
  theme(legend.position = "none") + 
  ylab("Drug liking (VAS 0-100)")

(p_posaffect | p_high | p_drug_like) +   
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") 
```

## Table for drug effects {#drugtab}

```{r TableDrugEffects}
summary_table.drug = 
  drug_effects.stats %>% 
  .[Outcome == "high", Outcome := "feeling_high"] %>% 
  .[grepl("high|like|dislike|dizzy|blunted", Outcome)] %>% 
  .[,Outcome := stringr::str_to_title(Outcome)] %>% 
  .[, Outcome := gsub("_"," ", Outcome)]

summary_table.drug %>% 
  my_flextable(caption = "Effects of drug manipulation measured at Q5 and Q6") 
```


```{r}
library(DT)

TC_table_drug = 
  rbind(p_posaffect$data[, Outcome := "Positve affect"],
        p_high$data[, Outcome := "High"],
        p_drug_like$data[, Outcome := "Drug liking"],
        fill = TRUE) %>% 
  .[, c("Activity_mid","Activity_start","grp","Activity") := NULL] %>% 
  .[, Manipulation := "Drug"]

TC_table_drug = TC_table_drug %>% 
  .[, mean_ci := sprintf("%.1f (%.1f, %.1f)", m, lower, upper)] %>% 
  setnames("state.condition","State") %>% 
  .[, .(State, Drug, Outcome, stage, Sex, mean_ci)]

datatable(
  TC_table_drug, 
  options = list(
    pageLength = 10,  # Number of rows per page
    search = list(regex = TRUE, smart = TRUE),  # Enables advanced search
    dom = 'ltipr',  # Includes search box for each column
    autoWidth = TRUE
  ), 
   caption = htmltools::tags$caption("Stage by stage results for drug manipulation check stratified by sex and state"),
  filter = "top"  # Adds search boxes at the top of each column
)

```

# Button presses in the self-administration task

As specified in the preregistration, we predicted that participants would use vigorous button presses to reach their target drug effect (self-reported desired drug effect).

We display the number of button presses participants made, stratified by drug condition and target effect. Participants with a target effect larger than 50% had to press buttons to subsequently receive a high dose during the second drug administration, while participants with a target effect lower than 50% had to press buttons to subsequently receive a low dose during the second drug administration.

This analysis excludes participants who indicated the opposite of the target they wanted to achieve, as these typically made very few button presses and were not following task instructions.

```{r ButtonsSelfAdmin}
dt.presses = 
  my_data %>% 
  .[cheater == FALSE] %>% 
  setkeyv(c("participant","session")) %>% 
  .[, last.trial := ifelse(time == max(time),TRUE,FALSE), by = .(session, participant)] %>% 
  .[, n.presses := p_down + p_up] %>% 
  .[, n.presses := cumsum(n.presses), by = .(session, participant)] %>% 
  .[, presses.per.s := n.presses/time] %>% 
  .[last.trial == TRUE] %>% 
  .[!is.na(satisfact)] %>% 
  .[, stage := 0] %>% 
  .[, N.oxy := sum(Drug == "oxycodone"), by = .(participant)] %>% 
  .[N.oxy > 1] %>% 
  .[, .(Sex, participant, session, Drug, 
        state.condition, target, n.presses, presses.per.s)] 


dt.presses[, `target effect` := ifelse(target > 50, "target > 50%","target < 50%")]

hist.presses = 
  dt.presses %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = presses.per.s, fill = Drug)) + 
  geom_histogram(position = "identity", alpha = .5, bins = 15) + 
  facet_grid(Sex~`target effect`) + 
  xlab("Number of presses second") + 
  ylab("Number of participants") +
  scale_col_drug

hist.presses

dt.presses %>% 
  .[, .(mean = round(mean(presses.per.s),1), sd = round(sd(presses.per.s),1), N = length(unique(participant))),
   by = .(Drug,state.condition)] %>% 
  my_flextable(caption = "Number of button presses per second by drug and stress condition and sex") 
```

```{r}
dt.presses %>% 
  .[, .(mean = round(mean(n.presses)), sd = round(sd(n.presses))),
   by = .(Drug,state.condition,Sex)] %>% 
  my_flextable(caption = "Number of button presses by drug and stress condition and sex") 
```

```{r, child=if (LONG_MODE) 'drug_want_like.Rmd'}
```


# Main Results

## Effect obtained

For analyses of the effect obtained in the self-administration task, we select only data from the oxycodone condition and load additional data with drug dosages.

```{r EffectObtained, eval=TRUE}
my_data[is.na(satisfact), last.trial := ifelse(time == max(time),TRUE,FALSE), by = .(session, participant)]

my_data_beh = 
  my_data[last.trial == TRUE & is.na(satisfact)] %>% 
  .[, stage := 0] %>% 
  .[, N.oxy := sum(Drug == "oxycodone"), by = .(participant)] %>% 
  .[N.oxy > 1] 

load("data/dosage_data.Rdata")
dosage_data %>% 
  .[, session := as.integer(session)] %>% 
  .[, weight_kg := as.numeric(gsub(",",".",weight_kg))]

for (v in c("weight_kg","drug_1_dose","drug_2_dose"))
  dosage_data[, (v) := as.numeric(gsub(",",".",get(v)))]

drug_feel = 
  my_data.Q %>% 
  .[item_name == "drug_feel" & Stage == "dose\n2", 
            .(session, participant, response, Drug, Stress)] %>% 
  setnames("response","drug_feel")

dosage_data = merge(dosage_data,drug_feel, by = c("participant","session"))


dosage_data[participant == 22 & session == 1, Drug := "oxycodone"]
feel_tresh = dosage_data[Drug == "placebo", quantile(drug_feel, .95)]
dosage_data = 
  dosage_data %>% 
   .[is.na(weight_kg), weight_kg := fmean(weight_kg), by = .(participant)] %>% 
  .[, session := as.integer(session)] %>% 
  setkeyv(.,key(my_data_beh)) %>% 
  .[participant %in% unique(my_data_beh$participant)]

mg_per_kg = fmean(dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone",drug_1_dose]/dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone",weight_kg])
dosage_data = 
  dosage_data %>% 
  merge(my_data_beh[, .(participant,session,target)], all = TRUE) %>% 
  .[is.na(drug_1_dose), dosage_data := weight_kg*mg_per_kg] %>% 
  .[, drug_2_dose_theory := drug_1_dose*(target/100)] %>% 
  .[, drug_1_dose_kg := drug_1_dose/weight_kg] %>% 
  .[, drug_2_dose_kg := drug_2_dose_theory/weight_kg] %>% 
  .[, drug_2_dose_70kg := drug_2_dose_kg*70] 


m.format = function(x, digits = 3) round(mean(x,na.rm = TRUE),digits)
```

### Self-administered drug dosages

```{r}
dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone" & drug_2_dose == 0, Dose2Omitted := "yes"]
dosage_data = 
  merge(dosage_data,
        my_data[last.trial == TRUE, .(participant, session, position)] %>% setnames("position","cursor%"),
        by = c("participant", "session"))

```


This section describes the drug dosages that participants achieved in the self-administration task.

The dose for the second drug administration was determined by the result of the self-administration task, whereby participant who had worked to obtain more than 100% of the effect of the sampling dose (i.e., first drug administration) still received the same dose as the sampling dose (i.e., 100%). In the stress condition, participants received on average `r m.format(dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone" & Stress == "stress", drug_2_dose_70kg])` mg during the second oxycodone administration (working for on average `r m.format(dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone" & Stress == "stress", target], digits=1)`%), whereas they received on average `r m.format(dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone" & Stress == "control", drug_2_dose_70kg])` mg (working for on average `r m.format(dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone" & Stress == "control", target], digits=1)`%) during the second oxycodone administration in the control condition. **Considering only those participants who wanted a non-zero dose**, the dose received during the second oxycodone administration were on average `r m.format(dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone" & Stress == "stress" & drug_2_dose > 0, drug_2_dose_70kg])` mg (working for on average `r m.format(dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone" & Stress == "stress" & drug_2_dose > 0, target], digits=1)`%) in the stress condition and `r m.format(dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone" & Stress == "control" & drug_2_dose > 0, drug_2_dose_70kg])` mg (working for on average `r m.format(dosage_data[Drug == "oxycodone" & Stress == "control" & drug_2_dose > 0, target], digits=1)`%) in the control condition. `r nrow(dosage_data[Dose2Omitted == "yes" & Drug == "oxycodone"])` participants opted out of receiving a second dose after having worked to obtain `r paste(round(sort(unlist(dosage_data[Dose2Omitted == "yes" & Drug == "oxycodone", "cursor%"]))), collapse="%, ")`% of the first effect. `r dosage_data[target == 0 & Drug == "oxycodone", participant] %>% length()` participants worked to obtain 0% during the second oxycodone administration in one of their sessions.

```{r SelfAdmin}
dosage_data %>% 
  .[, .(participant, Drug, Stress, drug_1_dose, drug_2_dose_theory,weight_kg)] %>% 
  melt(id.vars = c("participant","Drug","Stress","weight_kg"), value.name = "dose") %>% 
  na.omit() %>% 
  .[, `Dose nr` := ifelse(variable == "drug_1_dose", "Dose 1", "Dose 2")] %>% 
  .[, .(m = mean(dose/weight_kg), sd = sd(dose/weight_kg)), by = .(Drug,Stress, `Dose nr`)] %>% 
  .[, stat := paste0(m.format(m,3), " (",m.format(sd,3),")"), by = .(Drug,Stress, `Dose nr`)] %>% 
  dcast(Drug + Stress ~ `Dose nr`, value.var = "stat") %>% 
  setnames(c("Drug","Stress"), c("Drug", "State condition")) %>% 
  my_flextable(caption = "Drug dosages (mg/kg bodyweight) by drug and state condition.", footnote = "Values are means and standard deviations.") 
```

Unbeknownst to the participants, they only got at most 100% of the initial dose during the second drug administration. Three participants asked not to get a second dosage at all.


```{r}
dosage_data = 
  merge(dosage_data,unique(my_data.Q[, .(participant,Sex)]),
        by = "participant")
dosage_data %>% 
  .[, .(m = fmean(drug_2_dose/weight_kg), sd = fsd(drug_2_dose/weight_kg)),
            by = .(Drug, Stress, Sex)] %>% 
  na.omit() %>%
  my_flextable(caption = "Second drug dosages (mg/kg bodyweight) by drug and state condition.",digits = 3, footnote = "Values are means and standard deviations.") 
```

```{r}
dosage_data %>% 
  .[, .(m = fmean(target), sd = fsd(target)),
            by = .(Drug, Stress, Sex)] %>% 
  na.omit() %>%
  my_flextable(caption = "Target for self dministered second drug dosages (% of first) by drug and state condition.", footnote = "Values are means and standard deviations.") 
```


Effect of state induction and drug conditions on effect obtained in the self-administration task, stratified by Sex:

```{r, fig.cap="Mean and 95% confidence intervals for effect obtained, stratified by drug, state induction and sex. Note that these are calculated from the raw data."}
my_data_beh.stat = 
  my_data_beh %>% 
  .[, .(effect_obtained = mean(effect_obtained),
        se = sd(effect_obtained)/sqrt(.N),
        N = .N),
    by = .(state.condition,Drug,stage,Sex)] %>% 
  .[, `:=`(lower = effect_obtained-2*se,upper = effect_obtained+2*se)]

my_data_beh.stat %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = state.condition, y = effect_obtained, color = Drug, group = Drug)) + 
  geom_quasirandom(data = my_data_beh, alpha = .25) + 
  geom_line(position = position_dodge(.5)) +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(.5), size = 4) + 
  geom_linerange(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), position = position_dodge(.50)) + 
  facet_grid(Drug~Sex) +
  scale_col_drug + 
  ylim(0,126) +
  theme(panel.spacing = unit(2, "lines")) + 
  ylab("Effect obtained for the 2nd drug administration")
```

```{r fig.cap="Mean and 95% confidence intervals of  diffences in effect obtained between the stressful and the control state induction, stratified by drug condition (panels) and sex (colors). Note that these are calculated from the raw data."}
my_data_beh %>% 
  .[, .(participant,Sex, state.condition, effect_obtained, Drug)] %>% 
  dcast(participant + Drug + Sex ~state.condition, value.var = "effect_obtained", fun.aggregate = mean) %>% 
  .[, stress_effect := stress-control] %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = Sex, y = stress_effect, color = Sex, fill = Sex)) + 
  scale_col_sex + 
  stat_summary(fun.y = mean,alpha = .75,
               geom = "bar", color = NA) + 
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_normal, color = "black",
               geom = "errorbar", width = .25) + 
  facet_wrap(~Drug) + 
  geom_quasirandom(shape = 1, color = "black") + 
  theme(legend.position = "right")+ggtitle("Stress induction effect on obtained drug effect")+ylab("Stress effect: stress - control")
```

```{r fig.cap="Mean and 95% confidence interval for the interaction effect between drug and state induction conditions on effect obtained in the self-administration task (oxycodone (stress - control) - placebo (stress - control)), stratified by sex. Larger values indicate greater stress-induced wanting in the ocyxodone condition than in the placebo condition. Note that these are calculated from the raw data."}
my_data_beh %>% 
  .[, .(participant,Sex, state.condition, effect_obtained, Drug)] %>% 
  dcast(participant + Drug + Sex ~state.condition, value.var = "effect_obtained", fun.aggregate = mean) %>% 
  .[, stress_effect := stress-control] %>% 
  dcast(participant + Sex ~ Drug, value.var = "stress_effect") %>% 
  .[, stress_effect_x_drug := oxycodone - placebo] %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = Sex, y = stress_effect_x_drug, color = Sex, fill = Sex)) + 
  scale_col_sex + 
  stat_summary(fun.y = mean,alpha = .75,
               geom = "bar", color = NA) + 
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_normal, color = "black",
               geom = "errorbar", width = .25) + 
  geom_quasirandom(shape = 1, color = "black") + 
  theme(legend.position = "right")+ylab("Stress*Drug interaction")
```

As preregistered, we restrict the following analysis to the oxycodone condition.

```{r}
my_data_beh.all = 
  my_data_beh[, .(Sex, participant, session,
                  Drug, state.condition, effect_obtained, target,
                  mean.delta_effect_obtained,
                  first.time.target)] 
my_data_beh = 
  copy(my_data_beh.all) %>% 
  .[Drug == "oxycodone"]
```

To deal with the multimodal nature of the data, we use ordered logistic regression, for which we coarsen the data in 25 equally sized ordered categories.

```{r coarsen}
n_cat = 25
my_data_beh = 
  coarsen(my_data_beh,
          var = "effect_obtained",
          bins = n_cat,
          range = c(0,125))
```

### Bayesian Ordinal logistic regression (OLR) {#bayesian-ordinal-logistic-regression-olr}

As was the case for the questionnaire data, a linear regression model with Gaussian errors is not a good model for the effect obtained data (for details, see [Linear regression main outcome]). We therefore use Bayesian ordinal logistic regression models and fit these to coarsened into 20 equally sized bins. When reporting results, we back-transform effects (including credible intervals) of independent variables to the original 0-125% scale.

By implementing a hierarchical *cumulative* logistic models, we account for the multimodal distribution of the obtained drug effects and random effects of repeated measurements.The *cumulative* logistic regression model is often the default model for ordinal logistic regression, even though the required proportional odds assumptions might not always be met. For later analysis, we will use the *adjacent categories* ordinal logistic regression model, which allows us to relax the proportional odds assumption.

Even though the raw data show that the effect of stress is stronger in men, we first analyze the effects obtained data from the self-administration task without modelling sex differences. The ordinal regression model uses fixed effects parameters to model the effects of state induction condition and random effects to model varying intercepts and stress effects across participants, as well as effects of session number.

```{r BayesOLR, class.source = 'fold-show'}
analysis_formula = 
  effect_obtained.c ~ state.condition + 
                      (1 + state.condition | participant) +
                      (1|session)
```

We use brms' default priors for standard deviations of random effects (student-t with 3 df and a sd of 2.5) and a weakly informative normal prior for (fixed) main effects (i.e., normal distribution with a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 2). Priors for the thresholds are specified as normal distributions, with the logistic quantile function at cumulative probabilities of response categories as means, and with standard deviations of 1.

```{r nosexBRMS, results = "hide", message=FALSE}
my_data_beh[, session := factor(session)]
fn = "brmsP1/obtained/nosex.Rdata"
if (file.exists(fn)) {
  load(fn)
} else {
  my_prior = add_cumthresh_prior(
    prior(normal(0,2), class = b),
    my_data_beh$effect_obtained.c)
  fit = brm(
    analysis_formula,
    family = cumulative(),
    data = my_data_beh,
    prior = my_prior,
    backend = "cmdstanr",
    iter = 2000)
  save(fit,file = fn)
}
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(fit$data$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, fig.cap = "Summary of fit indicators. R-hat should be smaller than 1.01 and the effect sample size (ess) should be larger than 500.", fig.height=3}
sample_diags = fit %>% model_summary()
sample_diags$plot
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("<details> \n <summary>Click to show a table with the complete sample diagnostics.</summary>")
  cat(sample_diags$table)
  cat("</details> \n <br>")
}
```

The next figure shows that the model describes the observed data distribution well.

```{r fig.cap="Posterior predictive check for obtained effects.", fig.height=3}
pp_check(fit, ndraws = 2000, type = "bars")
```

```{r, echo = FALSE}
OR_avg_nosex = 
  fit$fit %>% 
  as_draws() %>% 
  subset_draws("b_state.condition", regex = TRUE) %>% 
  exp() 
```

### Bayesian OLR with adjacent category model with category specific effects

We repeat the previous analysis, with the exception that we switch to using an *adjacent category* ordered logistic model with category-specific effects. Unlike the previous *cumulative* model, this model does not assume proportional odds. The advantage of this model is that we can fit the data better and achieve higher statistical power to detect effects. The disadvantage of using this model, however, is that the model estimates separate odds ratios for each category transition rather than a single "common" odds ratio for all transitions.

Here we estimate the model:

```{r OLRAdjCat}
analysis_formula = 
  formula(effect_obtained.c ~ cs(state.condition) + (1 + state.condition | participant) + (1|session))
prior_sds = c(.5, 1, 5, 2)
make_prior_b = function(prior_sd) {
  return(
    eval(parse(text = paste("prior(normal(0,",prior_sd,"), class = b)")))
  )
}
for (prior_sd in prior_sds) {
  fn = paste0("brmsP1/obtained/nosex_acat",prior_sd,".Rdata")
  if (file.exists(fn)) {
    load(fn)
  } else {
    my_prior = 
      get_acat_thresh_priors(
        my_data_beh$effect_obtained.c,
        make_prior_b(prior_sd) + 
          prior(normal(0,2), class = "sd"))
    
    fit = brm(
      analysis_formula,
      family = acat(),
      data = my_data_beh,
      prior = my_prior,
      backend = "cmdstanr",
      iter = 2000)
    save(fit,file = fn)
  }
}
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(fit$data$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, fig.cap = "Summary of fit indicators. R-hat should be smaller than 1.01 and the effect sample size (ess) should be larger than 500.", fig.height=3}
sample_diags = fit %>% model_summary()
sample_diags$plot
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("<details> \n <summary>Click to show a table with the complete sample diagnostics.</summary>")
  cat(sample_diags$table)
  cat("</details> \n <br>")
}
```

Next, we calculate the effects obtained on the original 0-125% scale from the fitted model. This approach removes the effect of session by calculating model-predicted outcomes for the two stress conditions under the assumption that each condition could be in session 1, 2, 3, or 4.

```{r}
my_contrasts = plot_results_origscale(fit,dt = my_data_beh, ylim = c(60,120))
```

```{r, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of the estimated oxycodone drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right). Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
my_contrasts$plot
```

```{r}
my_caption = "Model-estimated effect obtained by condition"
my_contrasts$simple.stats %>% 
  my_flextable(caption = my_caption) 
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
cat(
  paste0(
    "Participants worked for on average ",
    round(my_contrasts$stats[,mean]),
    " (95% CrI=[",
    my_contrasts$stats[,CI], "]; ",
    "P(S>C)=",
    round(my_contrasts$stats[,"P > 0"],2),
    ") percentage points higher drug effect in the stress condition compared to the control condition.\n"
  )
)
```

### Prior sensitivity analysis

To assess the impact of the model priors on the results, we compare the effects calculated with the posterior distribution of the parameters with effects calculate from the prior distribution of the parameters.

Here, we calculate the same contrasts as we previously did for the posterior distributions, this time using samples generated from the prior distribution only.

```{r PriorSense}
psa_contrasts = c()
for (prior_sd in prior_sds) {
  fn = paste0("brmsP1/obtained/nosex_acat",prior_sd,"_psa.Rdata")
  if (file.exists(fn)) {
    load(fn)
  } else {
    my_prior = 
      eval(parse(text = paste("prior(normal(0,",prior_sd,"), class = b)")))+ 
      prior(normal(0,1), class = Intercept)
    prior_samples = brm(
      analysis_formula,
      sample_prior = "only", # only prior samples
      family = acat(),
      data = my_data_beh,
      prior = my_prior,
      backend = "cmdstanr",
      iter = 5000)
    save(prior_samples,file = fn)
  }
  # calculate contrasts
  my_contrasts_prior = plot_results_origscale(prior_samples,dt = my_data_beh)
  load(paste0("brmsP1/obtained/nosex_acat",prior_sd,".Rdata"))
  my_contrasts = plot_results_origscale(fit,dt = my_data_beh)
  
  # collect all samples
  psa_contrasts = rbind(
    psa_contrasts,
    rbind(
      my_contrasts$dt.samples.d[, .(value)][, from := "posterior"][, prior_sd := prior_sd],
      my_contrasts_prior$dt.samples.d[, .(value)][, from := "prior"][, prior_sd := prior_sd]
    )
  )
}
```

We compare stress effects from posterior and prior under the chosen prior of $\textrm{Normal}(0,2)$ for the main effect of state condition. Note that this effect does not simply follow a normal distribution as it is also influenced by other model parameters.

```{r fig.cap="Stress effect distribution under posterior samples and prior prior samples.", fig.height=3}
psa_contrasts %>% 
  .[, from := factor(from, levels = c("prior","posterior"))] %>% 
  .[prior_sd == 2] %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = value, color = from)) + 
  geom_density() + 
  xlab("calculated effect") +
  ylab("") +
  theme(legend.position = "right") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, lty = 2) + 
  gg_no_y_axis
```

The figure above illustrates that the prior distribution for the stress effect is a shrinkage prior that favors null effects over alternative effects. In comparison, the posterior distribution of the stress effect is narrower and has a non-zero mean. Using a shrinkage prior means that our estimate of the stress effect is conservative.

Next, are calculate stress effects (given the prior and posterior distribution) under alternative standard deviations for the prior of this stress effect:

```{r fig.cap="Stress effect distribution under posterior samples and prior prior samples when specifying alternative standard deviations for the prior of this stress effect."}
psa_contrasts %>% 
  .[, `prior sd` := factor(prior_sd, levels = c("2","0.5","1","5"))] %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = value, color = from, lty = `prior sd`)) + 
  geom_density() + 
  ylab("calculated effect") +
  xlab("") +
  facet_wrap(~from, scales = "free", nrow = 2) +
  theme(legend.position = "right") +
  gg_no_y_axis
```

```{r}
psa_contrasts %>% 
  .[, .(`mean (CI)` = get_mci(value,digits = 1)), by = .(from,prior_sd)] %>% 
  dcast(prior_sd ~ from) %>% 
  my_flextable(caption = "Effects of stress condition caculated from prior and posterior distribution.") 
```

Priors with smaller standard deviations for the effect of stress lead to posterior distributions for the effect of stress with somewhat smaller means and standard deviations, whereas priors with larger standard deviations for the effect of stress would lead to posterior distributions for the effect of stress with somewhat larger mean and standard deviations. In sum, our results show that we have employed shrinkage priors that result in a conservative estimate of the effect of stress.

### OLR with sex effects

Because the initial data visualization suggested sex effects, we estimate sex-specific main effects and effects of stress in the next model.

This analysis uses an adjacent category model with category-specific effects, which relaxes the proportional odds assumption. The model estimates the drug effect obtained in the self-administration task, dependent on state condition and sex, while using random effects to account for repeated measurement within individuals and for session effects.

We specify the model below:

```{r class.source = 'fold-show'}
analysis_formula = 
  effect_obtained.c ~ 
    # category specific effects of state condition and sex
    cs(state.condition*Sex) +
    # random effects for participants
    (1 + state.condition | participant) + 
    # random effects for session
    (1|session)
```

```{r OLRSexAcatBRMS, results = "hide", message=FALSE}
for (prior_sd in prior_sds) {
  fn = paste0("brmsP1/obtained/acat",prior_sd,".Rdata")
  if (file.exists(fn)) {
    load(fn)
  } else {
    my_prior = 
      get_acat_thresh_priors(
        my_data_beh$effect_obtained.c,
        make_prior_b(prior_sd) + 
          prior(normal(0,2), class = "sd"))
    control = list(adapt_delta = .90)
    fit = brm(
      analysis_formula,
      family = acat(),
      data = my_data_beh,
      prior = my_prior,
      backend = "cmdstanr",
      iter = 2000)
    save(fit,file = fn)
    prior_samples = update(fit,sample_prior = "only")
    save(prior_samples,file = gsub(".Rdata","_psa.Rdata",fn))
  }
}
load(paste0("brmsP1/obtained/acat",2,".Rdata"))
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(fit$data$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, fig.cap = "Summary of fit indicators. R-hat should be smaller than 1.01 and the effect sample size (ess) should be larger than 500.", fig.height=3}
sample_diags = fit %>% model_summary()
sample_diags$plot
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
if (rmd_output == "html") {
  cat("<details> \n <summary>Click to show a table with the complete sample diagnostics.</summary>")
  cat(sample_diags$table)
  cat("</details> \n <br>")
}
```

To communicate the results we calculate conditional stress effects, which show by how many percentage points the drug effect obtained in the self-administration task increased on average in the stress condition when compared to the control condition. See the function `make_plot_samples` in the utils.R file for details.

```{r}
plot.stats_table_obtained = 
  plot_results_origscale_sex2(
    fit,
    my_data_beh,
    outcome.var = "effect_obtained.c")

ggsave(plot.stats_table_obtained$plot,
        filename = "figures/Effect_Obtained.png", 
        width = 20,height = 15, units = "cm")
```

![Posterior distributions of the estimated oxycodone drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right), within and across men and women. Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals.](figures/Effect_Obtained.png){width="75%"}

```{r}
my_caption = "Model-estimated effect obtained stratified by sex and state condition."
my_footnote = "Numbers are means and 95% CrIs"
plot.stats_table_obtained$simple.stats %>% 
  my_flextable(digits = 2, caption = my_caption, footnote = my_footnote) 
```

```{r}
plot.stats_table_obtained$tbl_me_sex 
``` 

```{r}
my_caption = "Effect of stress on effect obtained across, within and between men and women"
my_footnote = "CI: Upper and lower bound of 95% credible intervals. P > 0: Posterior probability that the parameter or contrast is larger than 0."
plot.stats_table_obtained$stats[,.(Sex, mean, CI, `P > 0`)] %>% 
  .[, Sex := factor(Sex, levels = c("total","women","men","sex diff: m-f"))] %>% 
  setkeyv("Sex") %>% 
  my_flextable(digits = 2, caption = my_caption, footnote = my_footnote)

PS = plot.stats_table_obtained$stats
```

```{r results='asis', echo = FALSE}
cat(paste(
  "We used hierarchical ordinal logistic regression models to estimate the effect of stress on the obtained drug effect, which was measured as the percent of the drug effect from the first drug administration participants achieved through work in the self-administration task. To better understand the magnitude of these effects, we calculated marginal effects of stress on obtained drug effect. Taking sex differences into account revealed that while stress overall increased the effect obtained by",
  print_PSs(PS[3]),
  "points, this increase occurred primarily in men. In men, the effect obtained was",
  print_PSs(PS[1]),
  "percentage points higher in the stress compared to the control condition. In women, this difference was",
  print_PSs(PS[2]),
  ". The men-women differences of",
  print_PSs(PS[4]),
  "percentage points indicates that the effect of stress on desired drug effect was credibly higher in men than in women.\n\n",
  "To investigate potential explanations for the sex difference in obtained drug effect in the self-administration task, we explored sex differences in the effect of drug condition on positive and negative affect, embarrassment/selfconsciousness, and drug side effects (see \\@ref(effect-of-drug-on-post-state-induction-affect) [Effect of drug on post-state induction affect]). This exploratory analysis suggested that women reported greater side effects than men, but that there were no sex difference on the other subjective variables. Including side effects as a potential mediator in the model did not abolish the sex differences in the effect of stress on drug effect obtained in the self-administration task (see \\@ref(effect-obtained-after-adjusting-for-unpleasant-side-effects-at-q5-and-q6) [Effect obtained after adjusting for unpleasant side effects at Q5 and Q6]). This suggests that sex differences in drug effects on affect and side effects cannot fully explain the sex differences in stress effects on obtained drug effect in the self-administration task."
))
```

## Effect of drug on post-state induction affect

This section addresses some of the secondary hypotheses in the preregistration.

-   No. 1: "Pre-drug social stress will change opioid drug effects (e.g. mood, side effects, drug liking or disliking), compared to opioid drug effects in the no-stress condition. We do not have predictions as to the direction of any such changes."
-   No. 3: "Oxycodone will induce relief from negative affect and stress (from primary state induction) and block cortisol increases, compared to placebo."

The following plots show changes in affect from pre drug administration (stage 4) to post drug administration (stage 5) stratified by sex. Differences in pre-post slopes for the oxycodone and placebo conditions are indicative of drug effects of oxycodone on affect.

```{r DrugEffectOnPostIndAff}
my_data.QPS = 
  my_data.Q %>% 
  .[, Stage := factor(stage.names[stage], levels = stage.names)] %>% 
  .[stage %in% c(4,5) & 
              item_name %in% c(negaffect_items,
                               posaffect_items,
                               embselfcn_items)] %>% 
  .[, affect := ifelse(item_name %in% negaffect_items,
                       "negative",
                       ifelse(item_name %in% posaffect_items,
                              "positive","emb_selfc"))] %>% 
  coarsen(var = "response",
          bins = 20,
          range = c(0,100))
```

```{r, fig.height=3}
my_data.QPS_stats = 
  my_data.QPS %>% 
  .[, .(response = mean(response),
        se = sd(response)/sqrt(.N)),
    by = .(Drug, stage, affect, Sex)] %>% 
  .[, `:=`(lower = response-2*se,
           upper = response+2*se)] 

my_data.QPS_stats %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = stage, y = response, color = Drug,
             ymin = lower, ymax = upper, shape = Sex, lty = Sex)) +
  geom_point(position = pd.2) + 
  geom_line(aes(group = factor(Drug):factor(Sex)), position = pd.2) +
  geom_linerange(position = pd.2) + 
  facet_wrap(~affect) + 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(3.75,4.75), labels = c("state\ninduction","dose1"), limits = c(3.5,5.25)) +
  scale_col_drug

```

The means clearly indicate that affect did not develop more positively in the oxycodone condition compared to the placebo condition.

We can make the same comparison for side effects:

```{r}
my_data.QSE = 
  my_data.Q %>% 
  .[stage %in% c(4,5) & 
              item_name %in% sideffect_items] %>% 
  coarsen(var = "response",
          bins = 20,
          range = c(0,100))

my_data.QSE_stats = 
  my_data.QSE %>% 
  .[, .(response = mean(response),
        se = sd(response)/sqrt(.N)),
    by = .(Drug, stage, item_name, Sex)] %>% 
  .[, `:=`(lower = response-2*se,
           upper = response+2*se)] 

my_data.QSE_stats %>% 
    ggplot(aes(x = stage, y = response, color = Drug,
               ymin = lower, ymax = upper, shape = Sex, lty = Sex)) +
    geom_point(position = pd.2) + 
    geom_line(aes(group = factor(Drug):factor(Sex)), position = pd.2) +
    geom_linerange(position = pd.2) + 
    facet_wrap(~item_name) + 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(3.75,4.75), labels = c("state\ninduction","dose1"), limits = c(3.5,5.25)) +
  scale_col_drug
```

The means in this figure suggest that there was a greater increase in unpleasant side effects of oxycodone in women than in men.

## OLR placebo condition

The preregistered analysis of the obtained drug effect in the self-administration task was limited to the oxycodone condition. Here, we conduct a similar analysis, only for the placebo condition instead.

```{r }
my_data_beh_placebo = 
  copy(my_data_beh.all) %>% 
  .[Drug == "placebo"] %>% 
  .[effect_obtained > 125, effect_obtained := 125] %>% 
  coarsen(
    var = "effect_obtained",
    bins = n_cat,
    range = c(0,125))
analysis_formula = 
  formula(effect_obtained.c ~ state.condition:Sex + Sex + (1 + state.condition | participant) + (1|session))
```

We fit the model for effects obtained:

```{r OLRPlaceboBRMS, results = "hide", message=FALSE}
options(mc.cores = 4)
fn = "brmsP1/obtained/placebo.Rdata"
if (file.exists(fn)) {
  load(fn)
} else {
  my_prior = 
    get_acat_thresh_priors(
      my_data_beh_placebo$effect_obtained.c,
      make_prior_b(prior_sd) + 
        prior(normal(0,2), class = "sd"))
  fit = brm(
    analysis_formula,
    family = acat(),
    data = my_data_beh_placebo,
    prior = my_prior,
    backend = "cmdstanr",
    iter = 2000)
  save(fit,file = fn)
}
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(fit$data$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

<!-- ```{r fig.cap="Posterior predictive check"} -->

<!-- pp_check(fit, ndraws = 4000, type = "bars") -->

<!-- ``` -->

Next we make a figure for the placebo condition like we previously did for the oxycodone condition:

```{r, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of the estimated placebo drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right), within and across men and women. Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
plot.stats_table_obtained_placebo = 
  plot_results_origscale_sex2(
    fit,
    my_data_beh_placebo,
    outcome.var = "effect_obtained.c")

plot.stats_table_obtained_placebo$plot
```

## Sensitivity analyses

In this section, we test if the main results are robust to sample and covariate adjustments.

### Effect obtained only for those that wanted anything

In this sensitivity analysis, we limit the sample to only those participants who indicated that they wanted to achieve at least some drug effect (i.e., target > 0) by completing the self-administration task.

```{r EffectObtainedWanted, results = "hide", class.source = 'fold-show', message=FALSE, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of the estimated oxycodone drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right), limited to participants with a target drug effect > 0. Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
analysis_formula = 
  formula(effect_obtained.c ~ 
            cs(state.condition) + 
            (1 + state.condition | participant) + 
            (1|session))

result = 
  analyse_obtained(
  analysis_formula,
  my_data_beh[target > 0],
  "brmsP1/obtained/acat_want_nosex.Rdata",
  my_prior = NULL) 

result$plot
```

```{r}
my_caption = "Main results for analysis using only individuals with target > 0."
result$stats[, .(mean,CI,`P > 0`)] %>% my_flextable(digits = 2, caption = my_caption)
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(my_data_beh[target > 0]$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, results = "hide", class.source = 'fold-show', message=FALSE, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of the estimated oxycodone drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right), within and across men and women with a target drug effect > 0. Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
analysis_formula = 
  effect_obtained.c ~ 
    # category specific effects of state condition and sex
    cs(state.condition*Sex) +
    # random effects for participants
    (1 + state.condition | participant) + 
    # random effects for session
    (1|session)

result = 
  analyse_obtained(
  analysis_formula,
  my_data_beh[target > 0],
  "brmsP1/obtained/acat_want_sex.Rdata",
  my_prior = NULL) 

result$plot
```

```{r}
my_caption = "Main results for analysis using only individuals with target > 0, stratified by sex."
result$stats %>% my_flextable(digits = 2, caption = my_caption)
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(my_data_beh[target > 0]$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

### Effect obtained after adjusting for drug liking at Q5 and Q6

In this sensitivity analysis, we adjust for drug liking collected after the first drug administration and before the self-administration task.

```{r EffectsObtainedAdjusted}
adjustment = 
  my_data.Q %>% 
  .[stage %in% c(5,6) & item_name == "drug_like" & Drug == "oxycodone"] %>% 
  .[,.(liking = fmean(response)), by = .(state.condition, participant)]

merge_by = c("participant","state.condition")
my_data_beh = merge(my_data_beh,adjustment, by = merge_by, all.x = TRUE, all.y = FALSE)

adj = "drug liking"
```


```{r results = "hide", class.source = 'fold-show', message=FALSE, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of the estimated oxycodone drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right), after adjusting for drug liking. Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
analysis_formula = 
  formula(effect_obtained.c ~ 
            cs(state.condition) + liking + 
            (1 + state.condition | participant) + 
            (1|session))

result = 
  analyse_obtained(
  analysis_formula,
  my_data_beh,
  paste0("brmsP1/obtained/acat_adj_",adj,"_nosex.Rdata"),
  my_prior = NULL) 

result$plot
```

```{r}
my_caption = paste0("Main results for analysis adjusting for ",adj,".")
result$stats[, .(mean,CI,`P > 0`)] %>% my_flextable(digits = 2, caption = my_caption)
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(my_data_beh$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, results = "hide", class.source = 'fold-show', message=FALSE, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of the estimated oxycodone drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right), within and across men and women after adjusting for drug liking. Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
analysis_formula = 
  effect_obtained.c ~ 
    # category specific effects of state condition and sex
    cs(state.condition*Sex) + liking +
    # random effects for participants
    (1 + state.condition | participant) + 
    # random effects for session
    (1|session)

result = 
  analyse_obtained(
  analysis_formula,
  my_data_beh,
  paste0("brmsP1/obtained/acat_adj_",adj,"_sex.Rdata"),
  my_prior = NULL) 

result$plot
```

```{r}
my_caption = paste0("Main results for analysis adjusting for ",adj,", stratified by sex.")
result$stats %>% my_flextable(digits = 2, caption = my_caption)
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(my_data_beh$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

### Effect obtained after adjusting for stress ratings at Q5

In this sensitivity analysis, we adjust for stress ratings collected after the first drug administration and before the self-administration task.

```{r}
adjustment = my_data.Q[stage == 5 & item_name == "stressed" & Drug == "oxycodone"] %>% 
  .[,.(stressed = fmean(response)), by = .(state.condition, participant)]

merge_by = c("participant","state.condition")
my_data_beh = merge(my_data_beh,adjustment, by = merge_by, all.x = TRUE, all.y = FALSE)

adj = "stress"
```

```{r results = "hide", class.source = 'fold-show', message=FALSE, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of the estimated oxycodone drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right), after adjusting for stress ratings. Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
analysis_formula = 
  formula(effect_obtained.c ~ 
            cs(state.condition) + liking + 
            (1 + state.condition | participant) + 
            (1|session))

result = 
  analyse_obtained(
  analysis_formula,
  my_data_beh,
  paste0("brmsP1/obtained/acat_adj_",adj,"_nosex.Rdata"),
  my_prior = NULL) 

result$plot
```

```{r}
my_caption = paste0("Main results for analysis adjusting for ",adj,".")
result$stats[, .(mean,CI,`P > 0`)] %>% my_flextable(digits = 2, caption = my_caption)
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(my_data_beh$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, results = "hide", class.source = 'fold-show', message=FALSE, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of the estimated oxycodone drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right), within and across men and women after adjusting for stress ratings. Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
analysis_formula = 
  effect_obtained.c ~ 
    # category specific effects of state condition and sex
    cs(state.condition*Sex) + liking +
    # random effects for participants
    (1 + state.condition | participant) + 
    # random effects for session
    (1|session)

result = 
  analyse_obtained(
  analysis_formula,
  my_data_beh,
  paste0("brmsP1/obtained/acat_adj_",adj,"_sex.Rdata"),
  my_prior = NULL) 

result$plot
```

```{r}
my_caption = paste0("Main results for analysis adjusting for ",adj,", stratified by sex.")
result$stats %>% my_flextable(digits = 2, caption = my_caption)
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(my_data_beh$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

### Effect obtained after adjusting for unpleasant side effects at Q5 and Q6

In this sensitivity analysis, we adjust for ratings of unpleasant drug side effects collected after the first drug administration and before the self-administration task.

```{r EffectsObtainedAgjUnpleas}
adjustment = 
  my_data.Q %>% 
  .[stage %in% c(5,6) & item_name %in% sideffect_items & Drug == "oxycodone"] %>% 
  .[,.(sideeffects = fmean(response)), by = .(state.condition, participant)]

merge_by = c("participant","state.condition")
my_data_beh = merge(my_data_beh,adjustment, by = merge_by, all.x = TRUE, all.y = FALSE)

adj = "unpleasant side effects"
```


```{r results = "hide", class.source = 'fold-show', message=FALSE, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of the estimated oxycodone drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right), after adjusting for drug side effects. Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
analysis_formula = 
  formula(effect_obtained.c ~ 
            cs(state.condition) + sideeffects + 
            (1 + state.condition | participant) + 
            (1|session))

result = 
  analyse_obtained(
  analysis_formula,
  my_data_beh,
  paste0("brmsP1/obtained/acat_adj_",adj,"_nosex.Rdata"),
  my_prior = NULL) 

result$plot
```

```{r}
my_caption = paste0("Main results for analysis adjusting for ",adj,".")
result$stats[, .(mean,CI,`P > 0`)] %>% my_flextable(digits = 2, caption = my_caption)
```

```{r}
cat(paste0(length(unique(my_data_beh$participant)), " participants were included in this analysis."))
```

```{r, results = "hide", class.source = 'fold-show', message=FALSE, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of the estimated oxycodone drug effect obtained in the self-administration task in the two state induction conditions (left) and the effect of stress on this outcome calculate as stress condition minus control condition (right), within and across men and women after adjusting for drug side effects. Dots and vertical lines indicate means, and 50 & 95% credible intervals."}
analysis_formula = 
  effect_obtained.c ~ 
    # category specific effects of state condition and sex
